---
title: "Provincial Finances"
description: "What the provinces raise, spend, and owe — on a comparable basis"
body-classes: area-government
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CATEGORICAL_COLORS, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import category_bar, lines_over_time, province_colors

SHOW = {"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]}
prov_path = "data/government/statcan_provincial_finance.csv"
# West-to-east order, each province a fixed colour for the over-time chart.
PROV_ORDER = ["British Columbia", "Alberta", "Saskatchewan", "Manitoba", "Ontario",
              "Quebec", "New Brunswick", "Nova Scotia", "Prince Edward Island",
              "Newfoundland and Labrador"]
PROV_COLORS = province_colors(PROV_ORDER)  # locked identities (config PROVINCE_COLORS)
```

The federal government is only half of Canada's public finances: the provinces deliver health care, education, and most social services, and they raise and borrow accordingly. This page compares the ten provinces on a **consistent basis** — Statistics Canada's Canadian Government Finance Statistics (CGFS) — rather than each province's own public accounts, which use differing conventions. **Because of that, the figures here will not match a province's own budget documents**; they are built for comparison, not for a province's bottom line. Everything is shown as a share of each province's GDP. (The three territories are left out: they are small and largely federally funded, which would distort a share-of-GDP comparison.)

## How much each province owes

Net debt — what a government owes after subtracting its financial assets — is the broadest measure of a province's accumulated fiscal position. As a share of GDP it varies widely: resource-rich **Alberta** carries almost none, while **Ontario**, **Manitoba**, and **Newfoundland and Labrador** carry the most.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(prov_path):
    pf = pd.read_csv(prov_path)
    latest = pf[pf["year"] == pf["year"].max()].copy()
    yr = int(pf["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(latest, "province", "net_debt_pct_gdp",
        xaxis_title="Net debt (% of provincial GDP)", value_fmt=".1f", ticksuffix="%",
        color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0], hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada CGFS 10-10-0017-01 + GDP 36-10-0222-01 (net financial worth basis) | {yr}")
    fig.show(config=SHOW)
else:
    print("Provincial finance data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## What provinces raise and spend

How big is each provincial government, and is it running a surplus or a deficit? Each row below runs from **revenue** to **expense** as a share of GDP. Where the bar reaches further right, the province is spending more than it takes in (a deficit); where revenue is to the right of expense, it is in surplus. The smaller provinces run larger governments relative to their economies — partly because federal transfers top up their revenue.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(prov_path):
    pf = pd.read_csv(prov_path)
    d = pf[pf["year"] == pf["year"].max()].copy()
    yr = int(pf["year"].max())
    d = d.sort_values("expense_pct_gdp")
    order = d["province"].tolist()
    figd = go.Figure()
    for _, r in d.iterrows():
        figd.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=[r["revenue_pct_gdp"], r["expense_pct_gdp"]],
            y=[r["province"]] * 2, mode="lines",
            line=dict(color="#cfcfcf", width=3), showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip"))
    figd.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["revenue_pct_gdp"], y=d["province"], mode="markers",
        name="Revenue", marker=dict(color="#59a14f", size=10),
        hovertemplate="Revenue: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra>%{y}</extra>"))
    figd.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["expense_pct_gdp"], y=d["province"], mode="markers",
        name="Expense", marker=dict(color="#e15759", size=10),
        hovertemplate="Expense: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra>%{y}</extra>"))
    figd.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460,
        xaxis=dict(title="% of provincial GDP", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%"),
        yaxis=dict(title="", categoryorder="array", categoryarray=order),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        margin=dict(l=10, r=20, t=50, b=70))
    figd.add_annotation(text=f"Source: Statistics Canada CGFS 10-10-0017-01 + GDP 36-10-0222-01 | {yr}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.13, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figd.show(config=SHOW)
```

## Net debt over time

Provincial debt burdens have moved apart over the years. Alberta swung from net *assets* to modest debt as resource revenue fell; Newfoundland and Labrador's burden rose sharply; most others have been comparatively steady. (Use the legend to isolate a province.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(prov_path):
    pf = pd.read_csv(prov_path)
    fig = lines_over_time(pf, "year", "net_debt_pct_gdp", "province",
        yaxis_title="Net debt (% of provincial GDP)", colors=PROV_COLORS,
        group_order=PROV_ORDER, yticksuffix="%", hover_decimals=1, rangemode="normal",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada CGFS 10-10-0017-01 + GDP 36-10-0222-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(prov_path)}")
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color="#888", line_width=1.5)
    fig.show(config=SHOW)
```

## Download data

- [Provincial government finances, CGFS basis (CSV)](../data/government/statcan_provincial_finance.csv) — Statistics Canada 10-10-0017-01 + 36-10-0222-01
